text: The speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT) is one of the most pervasive phenomena in behavioral science, characterizing choice behavior across a vast taxonomic range—from insects and rodents to primates. The principle that decision speed and accuracy are inextricably linked is a fundamental property of goal-directed action. In recent years, the SAT has seen a resurgence of interest as neuroscientific techniques begin to map its physiological substrates and computational models adopt it as a pivotol benchmark for validity. This review provides a comprehensive examination of the SAT through three lenses. First, I trace its historical development as an empirical window into the "velocity of thought" and its role in shaping mathematical models of sequential information sampling. Second, I offer a practical "users guide" to SAT methodology, critically evaluating experimental manipulations—such as response deadlines, payoffs, and titration—alongside the analysis of behavioral signatures like conditional accuracy functions and quantile-probability plots. Finally, I review the application of these tools across diverse domains, including neurophysiology, pharmacology, and the study of cognitive pathology.
